feedback.text: Payment processors, notably MasterCard and PayPal are currently censoring what consumers can buy by pressuring commercial entities (Valve, Itch.io, among others) through acquiring banks. GamingOnLinux contacted valve following the delisting of games of a specific genre (source: https://www.gamingonlinux.com/2025/07/valve-gets-pressured-by-payment-processors-with-a-new-rule-for-game-devs-and-various-adult-games-removed/) Citing their rule 5.12.7 "Illegal or Brand-damaging Transactions", MasterCard forced Steam and Itch.io to remove perfectly legal games from the respective stores (source: https://www.pcgamer.com/games/mastercard-deflects-blame-for-nsfw-games-being-taken-down-but-valve-says-payment-processors-specifically-cited-a-mastercard-rule-about-damaging-the-brand/). In itself, this rule is already a massive issue regarding censorship since MasterCard reserves the right to prevent any transaction that may "reflect negatively on the Marks", for exemple, as cited in the rule, "The sale of a product or service, including an image, which is patently offensive and lacks serious artistic value". Obviously they do not define what is considered offensive, nor what artistic value is. This rule is a blanket statement that allows them to remove any content they or their CEO dislike. This led to Steam updating their rules and guidelines (source: https://partner.steamgames.com/doc/gettingstarted/onboarding). Under "Rules and Guidelines", answering the question "What you shouldnt publish on Steam", the rule n°15 answers: "Content that may violate the rules and standards set forth by Steams payment processors and related card networks and banks, or internet network providers. In particular, certain kinds of adult only content." Later, PayPal notified Valve (Developer and owner of the software distribution platform Steam) that their acquiring bank for payment transactions in certain currencies was immediately terminating the processing of any transactions related to Steam. Which means PayPal will no longer be usable as a payment method for every currency other than EUR, CAD, GB, JPY, AUD and USD (source: https://help.steampowered.com/en/faqs/view/731C-13C7-7D04-A11E#purpp). This isn't the first time MasterCard has shown their controlling practices over consumers. In 2020 Mastercard barred the use of its credit cards on Pornhub, an online pornography site (source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mastercard?utm_source=chatgpt.com#Other_issues). There is much more to tell about this issue but the main take away is: This is not about porn games, or horror games, or any specific kind of content. Everything censored was perfectly legal. This is about Visa, MasterCard and PayPal's monopoly over payment processing, and their ability to control what billions of people are allowed to buy and/or sell.
